fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the WASM runtime loader library. Basically the idea is that devs hand us a raw binary blob and we handle all the ugly plumbing — wiring up their callbacks, letting them read/write module state, and catching every possible way things can go wrong so it surfaces as something meaningful instead of a crash. We had a similar pattern in that import-validation flow we built for the scripting sandbox project — same idea where you pre-check everything before actually running. The big complaints we keep hearing are: (1) when something goes wrong with a binary or a missing hook, people get totally opaque native faults with no idea what happened; (2) numeric values going across the boundary come back mangled or lose precision; (3) stdout from the module just disappears. We also need the memory side to be safe — oversized allocations should fail clean. There's also the question of what happens when a host callback itself throws — that needs to bubble up properly rather than silently swallowing. The execution adapter piece that reads test scenarios and drives all this should stay completely separate from the core library logic. Make sure the overall code is organized by responsibility — don't dump everything in one file. Refer to the existing test scenarios to understand the expected wire format for outputs.

One more pass on the output behavior since a few folks asked for the exact shape. When compilation fails, like a bad magic number case, the adapter must emit exactly two lines: `error=compile_failed` first, then `detail=<runtime validation message>` second. That detail needs to carry the runtime’s raw message as-is, for example `Validation error: Bad magic number (at offset 0)` followed by a null byte `\0`. On function calls, wrong arity and wrong scalar type need to stay separate: wrong number of arguments emits `error=argument_count` and nothing else, and wrong scalar type emits `error=argument_type`, also as a single line.

Also, the export-name check is just a lookup result, not a failure path. If the name exists, emit `lookup[<name>]=present`; if it does not, emit `lookup[<name>]=missing`. No `error=` line for that. Same idea on globals: `read_global` for an exported global emits `global[<name>]=<value>`, `read_import_global` for an imported global emits `global[<ns>::<name>]=<value>`, and a successful `write_global` should just re-report the updated value in the same read format, so `global[<name>]=<new_value>`.

On the formatting side, float output needs to match the actual width crossing the boundary. 32-bit float results must render at single-precision fidelity, so IEEE-754 float32 rounding is what shows up. The concrete example here is 1234.5678 + 8765.4321 as f32 rendering as `9999.9990234375`, not `9999.9999`. For f64, keep full double precision, so those same inputs as f64 render as `9999.9999`. And if the scenario asks for `report_build: true` and instantiation succeeds, the adapter should include `instantiated=true` as a line in the output, but only when `report_build` explicitly asks for it.

On structure, we do want to be pretty strict about separation of responsibility. Binary compilation, import collection/validation, instantiation, typed value marshaling, memory management, globals, and the WASI/stdout layer should live in distinct units, and the execution adapter that does JSON stdin/stdout needs to stay completely separate from the core library. The core side should expose an idiomatic object API around module, instance builder, instance, functions, globals, and memory, with no coupling to JSON or stdio. And for the import pre-check behavior we talked about before, keep the same validation pass before instantiation for all required imports being present, kind mismatches, unknown import names, and duplicate fills, with each one mapping to its own distinct error category.

trigger_keywords: void return representation
function returns nothing output
how is void rendered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A function with no return value must be reported as `result[<fn>]=void` — the literal string `void`, not `0`, not an empty string, not omitted. This distinguishes a side-effecting call from a function that returns a numeric zero.

trigger_keywords: host import receives args
echo args from module call
import_call step output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: After a module calls a host import, an `import_call` step queries what arguments that import received. The output line is `import_call[<namespace>::<name>]=<v1>,<v2>,...` with values comma-separated and no spaces. For example: `import_call[env::report]=123,456`.

trigger_keywords: host import throws exception
error from host callback
imported function error propagation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a host import declared with `throw` behavior is called by the module, the error must unwind the in-progress `invoke` and be reported as `error=imported_function_threw` followed by `message=<the error message text>` on the next line. Control must not continue past the throw.

trigger_keywords: writing to a constant global
const global error
immutable global write
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Attempting to write to a constant (imported, non-mutable) global must emit `error=const_global_write` (one line, no additional fields). Attempting to write a value whose type does not match the global's declared scalar type must emit `error=bad_value_type` followed by `type=<declared type name>` on the next line (e.g. `type=int32`).

trigger_keywords: memory size output format
pages and bytes reporting
memory info step
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The memory `info` op emits two lines: `pages=<n>` then `bytes=<n>`. A write op emits `write[<index>]=<value>`. A read op emits `byte[<index>]=<value>`. A grow op on success emits `grow[<n>]=ok`. Failure to create memory emits `error=memory_create_failed`; failure to grow emits `error=memory_grow_failed`.

trigger_keywords: import validation errors
missing import at instantiation
duplicate import filling
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Import validation produces distinct error categories: `error=missing_import` when a required import was not supplied; `error=import_kind_mismatch` followed by `expected=<kind>` when the supplied import is the wrong kind (e.g. global supplied where function expected); `error=import_not_found` followed by `import=<ns>::<name>` when the supplied import names a module/name pair that does not exist in the module's import section; `error=import_already_filled` when the same import slot is filled twice.

trigger_keywords: wasi stdout capture
system interface enable
wasi fill failed error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Enabling the WASI system interface on a module that does not have the required `wasi_unstable::fd_write` import must emit `error=wasi_fill_failed`. Enabling it twice on the same builder must emit `error=wasi_already_enabled`. Building a module that imports WASI functions without enabling WASI must emit `error=missing_import`. Requesting captured stdout or stderr when capture was not enabled must emit `error=stdout_without_wasi` or `error=stderr_without_wasi` respectively. Captured output is reported as `output=<text>` or `stdout=<text>`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the existing test scenarios to understand the expected wire format for outputs
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ (features 1–9), whose `expected_output` strings define the exact line-oriented text format the execution adapter must produce for every operation result and error category.

context_pointers.vague_reference: stdout from the module just disappears
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 9 (feature9_1_wasi_capture_stdout.json, feature9_2_manual_fd_write.json, feature9_3_wasi_errors.json): the WASI system interface layer that intercepts fd_write calls and accumulates captured output, returned as `output=<text>` or `stdout=<text>` lines.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — please check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the PRD in start.md for the authoritative contract. If you're seeing unexpected output, compare it character-for-character against the `expected_output` strings in the relevant feature's JSON file.

feature1_module_compilation.json.description: Compiling a byte buffer into a module: a buffer whose leading bytes are not the WASM binary signature is rejected with a normalized compile-failure category and the underlying validation detail.

cases.expected_output:
error=compile_failed
detail=Validation error: Bad magic number (at offset 0)�

feature2_function_invocation.json.description: Looking up an exported function by name and calling it; looking up a name that is not exported reports it as missing; calling with the wrong number or wrong type of arguments yields normalized argument errors.

feature3_numeric_types.json.description: Calling functions that add the four scalar value types (32/64-bit integers and 32/64-bit floats) and returning the wire result for each.

feature4_void_and_noarg.json.description: Calling a function with a void return type (which reports no value) followed by a parameterless function that returns the state mutated by the first call.

feature5_1_host_function_args.json.description: A module function that calls back into a host-provided function; the host import records the arguments it receives, demonstrating values flow from module code into the host callback.

feature5_2_host_function_exception.json.description: When a host-provided import raises an error during a module call, the error propagates out of the call and is reported with a normalized category plus the error message.

feature6_globals.json.description: Reading exported and imported globals, calling a function that depends on them, writing a mutable global and observing the dependent function change, then the errors raised by writing a constant global and by writing a value of the wrong type.

cases.expected_output:
global[foo]=37
global[env::bar]=10
result[baz]=370
global[foo]=99
result[baz]=990
error=const_global_write
error=bad_value_type
type=int32

feature7_1_memory_ops.json.description: Creating a standalone linear memory, reporting its size in pages and bytes, writing and reading a byte, growing it by some pages, and confirming size grew and the written byte survived the grow.

cases.expected_output:
pages=100
bytes=6553600
write[123]=45
byte[123]=45
grow[10]=ok
pages=110
bytes=7208960
byte[123]=45

feature7_2_memory_errors.json.description: Memory operations that exceed limits: requesting an impossibly large initial size fails creation; growing past the implementation limit fails; growing past a declared maximum fails. Each yields a normalized category.

feature8_import_validation.json.description: Instantiation-time import checking: a fully and correctly supplied set of imports instantiates successfully; leaving an import unfilled, supplying an import of the wrong kind (memory/global where a function is expected), naming an import that does not exist, or filling the same import twice each yields a normalized category (with the offending import name where relevant).

feature9_1_wasi_capture_stdout.json.description: Enabling the system interface with stdout capture, running the module entry point, and reading back the captured standard output text.

feature9_2_manual_fd_write.json.description: Supplying a host write function manually (instead of enabling the system interface): the module entry point calls it, the host reads the strings from the instance memory and accumulates them, and the accumulated output is reported.

feature9_3_wasi_errors.json.description: System-interface error conditions: enabling it for a module that lacks the required imports fails; enabling it twice fails; building a module that needs the interface without enabling it reports a missing import; and requesting captured stdout/stderr when capture was not enabled fails. Each yields a normalized category.
